CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (extensive comparison of 21 metrics, cross-system/dataset analysis, and the RAINBOW combined metric) are stated early and carried through to the conclusion. Writing quality is reported as good, related work coverage adequate, and figures/tables (metrics/dataset/system breakdown) appear to be informative for the reader. I deduct one point because the summary does not confirm whether notation and formal definitions (e.g. for the combined metric and GBMs) are presented with full consistency and clarity, and a stronger explicit statement of evaluation setup details (e.g. crowdsourcing agreement measures and hyperparameter reporting) would improve reproducibility and presentation. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear experimental flow (intro -> methods -> experiments -> conclusion) and explicitly states its main contributions early (comparison of 21 metrics, three systems/datasets, and the RAINBOW metric). Notation and definitions appear consistent enough for reproducibility (code is released), and figures/tables/examples supporting the comprehensive comparison and error analysis are effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., some implementation/hyperparameter details not reported in the summary) that prevent it from being rated exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (extensive comparison of 21 metrics, cross-system/dataset analysis, and the RAINBOW combined metric) are stated early and carried through to the conclusion. Writing quality is reported as good, related work coverage adequate, and figures/tables (metrics/dataset/system breakdown) appear to be informative for the reader. I deduct one point because the summary does not confirm whether notation and formal definitions (e.g. for the combined metric and GBMs) are presented with full consistency and clarity, and a stronger explicit statement of evaluation setup details (e.g. crowdsourcing agreement measures and hyperparameter reporting) would improve reproducibility and presentation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear experimental flow (intro -> methods -> experiments -> conclusion) and explicitly states its main contributions early (comparison of 21 metrics, three systems/datasets, and the RAINBOW metric). Notation and definitions appear consistent enough for reproducibility (code is released), and figures/tables/examples supporting the comprehensive comparison and error analysis are effective and informative. Writing quality is good overall, with only minor presentation issues (e.g., some implementation/hyperparameter details not reported in the summary) that prevent it from being rated exceptional.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper offers a notable novel contribution by introducing new grammar-based metrics and a combined metric (RAINBOW), and by presenting the first sentence-level comparison of 21 automated metrics across multiple end-to-end NLG systems and datasets. While evaluation of automatic NLG metrics is a well-studied problem, the breadth of metrics compared and the proposal of a combined metric that substantially improves correlation (ρ up to 0.81) represent a clear, differentiated addition beyond prior empirical work. From a theory-first perspective I would have liked a formal justification for the combination strategy, so I view the contribution as notable but not a fundamentally new theoretical perspective, hence a 4/5. | B(4): The paper provides notable novelty by assembling and evaluating a very broad set of 21 metrics—including newly introduced grammar-based metrics—on sentence-level NLG across three systems and three datasets, which to my knowledge is a broader, more focused empirical comparison than prior work. The proposed combined metric RAINBOW that substantially improves sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) represents a clear methodological contribution beyond merely reporting comparisons. While the work is empirical rather than introducing radically new theory, the breadth of metrics, the novel GBMs, and the combined metric together constitute a meaningful and practically useful advance that would inform experts and practitioners.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper offers a notable novel contribution by introducing new grammar-based metrics and a combined metric (RAINBOW), and by presenting the first sentence-level comparison of 21 automated metrics across multiple end-to-end NLG systems and datasets. While evaluation of automatic NLG metrics is a well-studied problem, the breadth of metrics compared and the proposal of a combined metric that substantially improves correlation (ρ up to 0.81) represent a clear, differentiated addition beyond prior empirical work. From a theory-first perspective I would have liked a formal justification for the combination strategy, so I view the contribution as notable but not a fundamentally new theoretical perspective, hence a 4/5.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides notable novelty by assembling and evaluating a very broad set of 21 metrics—including newly introduced grammar-based metrics—on sentence-level NLG across three systems and three datasets, which to my knowledge is a broader, more focused empirical comparison than prior work. The proposed combined metric RAINBOW that substantially improves sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81) represents a clear methodological contribution beyond merely reporting comparisons. While the work is empirical rather than introducing radically new theory, the breadth of metrics, the novel GBMs, and the combined metric together constitute a meaningful and practically useful advance that would inform experts and practitioners.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree this is a solid, broad empirical study with useful error analysis and released code that supports the paper’s main claims, but it lacks key analyses and reporting (no sensitivity/ablation of RAINBOW, limited datasets/systems, and incomplete crowdsourcing/hyperparameter details). These omissions limit claims of generality and full reproducibility but do not outweigh the paper’s substantive empirical contributions, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The study presents a reasonably thorough empirical comparison (21 metrics, 3 systems, 3 datasets) and includes error analysis and explicit limitations, but from a theory-first perspective it falls short: the new combined metric RAINBOW lacks a formal justification or analytic guarantees and no sensitivity/ablation analyses of its weighting or cross-domain stability are reported. The evaluation sample is also limited (only 3 systems and 3 datasets, below my five-dataset/system expectation for claims of generality), and crowdsourcing details (inter-annotator agreement, qualification filters) and some experimental hyperparameters are not reported despite code availability—reducing reproducibility confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper presents a broad, well-executed empirical study (21 metrics including novel grammar-based ones) across three NLG systems and three datasets with crowdsourced sentence-level human judgments, which strongly supports the core claims about metric weaknesses and the value of a combined metric (RAINBOW). It also includes error analysis and releases code, aiding reproducibility and practical impact — points I prioritize. The main gaps are absence of reported hyperparameters and no explicit ablation or sensitivity analysis of RAINBOW’s components mentioned in the summary, which would be important to fully validate its robustness across datasets/systems. Overall the experiments are solid and comprehensive with minor missing analyses.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong topical fit for the NLG/evaluation community, provides a comprehensive empirical comparison across many metrics, and is practically useful with released code. Reviewer A’s concerns about limited theoretical justification for the combined metric, missing ablation/sensitivity analyses, and incomplete crowdsourcing detail warrant caution about claiming broad generality. Given the solid empirical gains and reproducibility but the methodological gaps that should be addressed, a slightly above‑borderline score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: Topical fit is good — this is an NLG evaluation paper aimed squarely at the NLP community and addresses an important problem (automatic metrics vs. human judgments) with code released and extensive empirical comparisons across 21 metrics and three datasets/systems. However, from a theory-first review perspective it falls short: the proposed combined metric (RAINBOW) lacks formal justification, ablation/sensitivity analyses or cross-domain validation that would explain why it should generalize beyond the three datasets and three systems. The empirical scope is also limited for strong venue claims (only 3 systems and 3 datasets, fewer than the 5+ diverse datasets/systems I expect), and the summary omits key crowdsourcing details (IAA, worker qualifications) and hyperparameter/stability reporting, which weakens confidence in the human baseline and metric reliability. Given strong relevance but these methodological and theoretical gaps, I judge it borderline appropriate for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP venue: it tackles a core NLG evaluation problem with a comprehensive empirical study (21 metrics including novel grammar-based metrics, three systems RNNLG/TGEN/LOLS, and three datasets SFHOTEL/SFREST/BAGEL) and proposes a new combined metric (RAINBOW) with strong sentence-level improvements (ρ up to 0.81). The work targets the right audience (NLG and evaluation researchers/practitioners), follows community standards (human Likert judgments, correlation analyses, error analysis), and provides practical impact and reproducibility (code released). The stated limitations are appropriate and do not undermine its relevance or placement at a top conference.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical problem and provides a valuable empirical resource showing that many existing metrics fail at sentence level and that a combined metric (RAINBOW) substantially improves correlation; public code release strengthens reproducibility. Concerns about limited datasets/systems, lack of formal justification, and missing crowdsourcing/robustness details temper claims of universal applicability, so while the work is likely to change evaluation practice and spur follow-ups, it is not yet transformative across all settings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—reliability of automatic NLG metrics—and provides a useful empirical resource by comparing 21 metrics (including novel GBMs) and proposing a combined metric (RAINBOW) with strong reported sentence-level correlation (ρ up to 0.81). However, from a theory-first perspective its impact is limited: the new combined metric lacks formal justification or analytic guarantees and the empirical evaluation is narrow (only three datasets and three systems, below the threshold I require for strong generalization claims). Crowdsourcing details (e.g. inter-annotator agreement) and robustness analyses for the combined metric are not reported in the summary, reducing confidence in broad applicability. Overall, useful and likely to spur follow-up work, but not yet transformative without stronger theoretical grounding and broader validation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a central, practical problem—reliably evaluating sentence-level NLG—which has direct impact on both research and deployment. The comprehensive empirical comparison (21 metrics including novel grammar-based metrics) across three systems and three datasets, plus publicly released code, provides strong, reproducible evidence that commonly used metrics fail at sentence-level and that a combined metric (RAINBOW) can substantially improve sentence-level correlation (up to ρ=0.81). While limitations on dataset- and system-specificity temper claims of universal applicability, the work is likely to change evaluation practice and spur follow-up work, making it a strong-impact contribution.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides substantial empirical evidence and useful contributions (wide metric comparison, code release, improved correlations), but note limitations in theoretical justification, limited datasets/systems, and missing details (hyperparameters, ablation/sensitivity, inter-annotator agreement). These shortcomings are important but do not overturn the empirical findings, so a score of 4 reflects strong experimental support with some notable methodological gaps that prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents substantial empirical evidence (21 metrics across 3 systems and 3 datasets) supporting the main claim that many automatic metrics poorly reflect sentence-level human judgments, and the code is available — so experiments appear generally sound. However, from a theory-first perspective the new combined metric RAINBOW lacks formal justification or analytic guarantees and the paper evaluates on only three datasets/two domains and three systems (well below my preferred ≥5), weakening claims of generality. Methodological gaps include no report of crowdsourcing inter-annotator agreement or hyperparameter/weighting details for the combined metric (no ablation or sensitivity analysis), which undermines confidence in the robustness of results. Overall the work is useful empirically but has important technical shortcomings that prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main empirical claims are well-supported: it compares 21 metrics (including novel GBMs) across three systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) and three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL), collects human judgments, and shows RAINBOW substantially improves sentence-level ρ (up to 0.81). Experimental methodology is largely sound with appropriate baselines and extensive metric coverage, and code release aids reproducibility. Minor shortcomings limit perfection: hyperparameters are not reported, the Stanford parser is acknowledged as an imperfect grammaticality proxy, and the summary does not state whether appropriate statistical significance testing was applied, leaving some concerns about robustness and generalisability across domains/systems. Overall the technical evidence supports the claims, with only modest gaps that do not overturn the conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper performs a broad and fair empirical comparison (21 metrics, 3 datasets, 3 systems) and provides useful analyses and code. I dock one point for limited generalization (only three systems/domains) and for insufficient theoretical or sensitivity analysis fully justifying the proposed combined RAINBOW metric, which weakens claims of universal superiority despite strong empirical evidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against an extensive set of 21 established automatic metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, METEOR, CIDEr, etc.) and evaluates three real NLG systems on three datasets, so baselines are broadly appropriate and relevant. Comparisons appear fair (same datasets/systems/metrics) and the authors acknowledge when standard metrics perform reasonably at system-level but fail at sentence-level. However, I penalize for limited generalization scope (only 3 systems and 3 datasets across two domains) and for lack of a theoretical or sensitivity analysis justifying the combined RAINBOW metric, which weakens claims of superiority across diverse settings.

reviewer_b.justification: Comprehensive: the paper compares 21 automatic metrics (including novel grammar-based metrics) across three realistic datasets and three contemporary end-to-end NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS), which exceeds my threshold for breadth. Fairness is preserved — the same data and metrics were used across systems — and the authors openly acknowledge when existing metrics are reasonable at system-level and dataset/system-specific limitations. They also provide code and discuss related work adequately, and they analyze differences between RAINBOW and prior metrics through sentence-level correlation and error analysis.

main_claims: Automatic word-overlap and grammar-based metrics only weakly reflect human judgments for end-to-end data-driven NLG.
This study is the first to compare 21 automated metrics including novel grammar-based metrics (GBMs) on sentence-level NLG and to evaluate across three systems and three datasets (two domains).
They propose a new combined metric, RAINBOW, which shows much higher sentence-level correlation with human judgments (up to ρ=0.81) than existing metrics.
Automatic metrics can be reasonable at system-level but are unreliable at sentence-level, especially for utterances rated medium or good; metric performance is dataset- and system-specific.
_summary.methodology: Compare 3 end-to-end data-driven NLG systems (RNNLG, TGEN, LOLS) across three datasets (SFHOTEL, SFREST, BAGEL) using 21 automatic metrics (word-overlap and grammar-based), collect human Likert-scale ratings for informativeness, naturalness and quality via crowdsourcing, compute correlations and perform detailed error analysis, and propose a combined metric (RAINBOW).
limitations: Automatic metrics are not reliable at sentence-level and perform poorly distinguishing medium/good outputs.
Metric performance is data- and system-specific, limiting generalisability.
The Stanford parser score is not designed to measure grammaticality (not a perfect grammaticality measure).

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Critic
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a focus on formal evaluation theory and statistical learning theory; long-term reviewer for ACL and TACL.
traits: Penalizes empirical papers that do not provide a theoretical model or formal justification for new metrics (will deduct points if a new metric lacks analytic guarantees or an explanation of why it should correlate with human judgments).
Requires broad generalization evidence: deducts points if fewer than 5 diverse datasets or fewer than 5 systems are evaluated (believes small-sample empirical claims are fragile).
Strict about crowdsourcing methodology: penalizes papers that do not report inter-annotator agreement, qualification filters, or repeated-trials analysis for Likert ratings.

Suspicious of combined metrics without ablation/proven stability: will lower score if the combination method lacks sensitivity analyses, cross-validation across domains, or theory explaining feature weighting.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I prioritize principled, generalizable contributions supported by formal analysis; empirical results are necessary but insufficient without theoretical grounding and strong cross-domain validation.

reviewer_b.name: Applied-Empiricist Advocate
reviewer_b.background: Senior applied NLP researcher focused on evaluation and metric development for NLG systems; frequent reviewer and area chair for ACL/EMNLP conferences.
traits: Rewards comprehensive empirical studies: adds points for papers that compare many metrics (>=15) and include novel metric families (values breadth of evaluation).
Values practical impact and reproducibility: gives credit if code, data, and metric implementations are released or if experiments cover multiple realistic datasets/domains (>=2) and systems (>=3).

Encourages improvements in sentence-level evaluation: will raise score when a proposed metric demonstrably increases sentence-level correlation with human judgments (e.g., shows substantial ρ improvement).
Favors careful error analysis and actionable insights: adds credit when the paper identifies specific failure modes of existing metrics and provides diagnostics that practitioners can use.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize thorough, well-documented empirical work that moves practice forward by providing reliable evaluation tools and clear guidance for system developers.
